metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's nginx-config install recipe description: replace the `sed` reference with `envsubst`, identify the actual script (or create `infra/scripts/install-nginx-config.sh`) that performs the rendering, and specify how the DENY-ALL opcert-guard.conf default is installed as part of that script (not as an extension of the template header comment).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'lines 8-9 [of infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template] render the template via `sed`', but reading the file shows lines 7-10 document `envsubst` as the rendering tool — `sed` is never mentioned. The plan's boot-hazard closure argument ('the nginx-config install recipe … is EXTENDED with one more line that installs a DENY-ALL default') rests on this false premise about the mechanism. Furthermore, the header is illustrative documentation (a comment), not an executed script; there is no `install-nginx.sh` or equivalent script in `infra/scripts/` that actually runs the `envsubst` rendering today — so the 'recipe' the plan proposes to extend does not exist as an executable artifact.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring paragraph: 'the nginx-config install recipe — the documented header of infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template itself (its lines 8-9 render the template via sed into /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf; no separate script owns this today)'
findings.suggested_fix: Create `infra/scripts/install-nginx-config.sh` that runs `envsubst '${REVIEW_BOT_PORT}' < infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template > /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf` and also writes the DENY-ALL `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf`; reference this script explicitly in the plan as the artifact to extend/create, and wire it into compose-up.sh before materialize-opcert-guard.sh.

findings.finding: AC1 provides a concrete jq proving command only for the IAM invoke-policy sub-claim; the remaining AC1 assertions — (a) https:// integration URI, (b) X-Opcert-Guard static request-header mapping on the integration, (c) /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key SecureString with lifecycle ignore_changes, (d) /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard SecureString under the existing path grant, (e) hashicorp/random in required_providers — are covered only by the vague phrase 'A test asserts these declarations exist' with no stated command or query.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 (second and third sub-bullets after the jq block)
findings.suggested_fix:
Extend the AC1 test block with additional `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e` assertions, e.g.:
• URI: `... | select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") | .values.integration_uri | startswith("https://")`
• Guard header: `... | select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") | .values.request_parameters["overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard"]`
• Key lifecycle: `... | select(.type=="aws_ssm_parameter" and .values.name=="/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key") | .values.lifecycle.ignore_changes | contains(["value"])`
• Random provider: `grep -E '"random"' infra/terraform/.terraform.lock.hcl` (post-init) or check versions.tf directly.

findings.finding: The plan's boot-ordering section states the nginx-config install step 'ALSO installs a DENY-ALL default /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf' but the step that installs rebar.conf from the template to /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf is not owned by any existing script in the repo (install-certbot-timer.sh references it only in a comment; compose-up.sh does not install it). The plan piggybacks on this 'nginx-config install step' without naming the script/runbook step that owns it or declaring it as an edit target, leaving the deny-all guard install unrooted.
findings.location: What section / Compose wiring / boot ordering hazard (a)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Provide the complete, literal `jq -e` command for each of the five remaining AC1 sub-claims (https integration URI scheme, X-Opcert-Guard static request-parameter key, both SecureString parameter types/names, AWS_IAM route authorization_type, and lifecycle.ignore_changes=["value"] for the key parameter) so every assertion is self-contained and falsifiable without executor guesswork.
findings.finding: AC1 asserts that 'EVERY AC1 sub-claim is asserted the same structured way — a `jq -e` query per sub-claim against the one `terraform show -json tf.plan` output' for at least five distinct sub-claims (the `https://` integration URI scheme, the `X-Opcert-Guard` static request-parameter mapping, the two SecureString parameters and their names, the `AWS_IAM` route authorization, and the key parameter's `lifecycle.ignore_changes` block), but only the Invoke-policy assertion command is written out in full. An executing agent must derive the remaining jq commands independently, with no guarantee they use the correct JSON key paths or pass the criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, second paragraph beginning 'EVERY AC1 sub-claim is asserted the same structured way'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add the following concrete commands to AC1 (verify exact provider key names against `terraform show -json` output on a test plan before committing):
```bash
# https:// integration URI
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '
  [.planned_values.root_module.resources[]
   | select(.type == "aws_apigatewayv2_integration")
   | select(.values.integration_uri | startswith("https://"))]
  | length >= 1'

# X-Opcert-Guard static request parameter
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '
  [.planned_values.root_module.resources[]
   | select(.type == "aws_apigatewayv2_integration")
   | .values.request_parameters
   | to_entries[]
   | select(.key | test("X-Opcert-Guard"; "i"))]
  | length == 1'

# Both SecureString parameters with correct names
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '
  [.planned_values.root_module.resources[]
   | select(.type == "aws_ssm_parameter" and .values.type == "SecureString")
   | .values.name]
  | (contains(["/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key"]) and contains(["/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard"]))'

# AWS_IAM route authorization_type
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '
  [.planned_values.root_module.resources[]
   | select(.type == "aws_apigatewayv2_route")
   | select(.values.authorization_type == "AWS_IAM")]
  | length >= 1'

# lifecycle ignore_changes = ["value"] on the key parameter
terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '
  [.configuration.root_module.resources[]
   | select(.type == "aws_ssm_parameter")
   | select(.expressions.name.constant_value == "/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key")
   | select(.lifecycle.ignore_changes | contains(["value"]))]
  | length == 1'
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `jq -e` sub-claim to AC1's structured assertion block that verifies the `rebar-opcert-admin` role's `assume_role_policy` is non-empty and does NOT contain a wildcard principal (`'Principal': '*'` or `'AWS': '*'`), and add a `validation {}` block to the `opcert_admin_principal_arns` variable that rejects empty lists and wildcard entries.
findings.finding: The AC1 structured jq -e assertion verifies only the permission policy (that exactly one execute-api:Invoke grant exists with label 'opcert_admin_invoke') but does NOT verify the rebar-opcert-admin role's trust policy is actually restricted to the principal ARNs in opcert_admin_principal_arns. A misconfigured or overly-broad trust policy (e.g., trusting 'Principal: *' or the entire AWS account) would allow any authenticated AWS identity to assume the role and call Invoke — the boundary-crossing sensitive path has an undeclared/unverified access posture on the trust-policy dimension.
findings.location: AC1 — structured jq assertion / 'What' section: 'trust policy limited to the principal ARNs supplied via a new variable opcert_admin_principal_arns'
findings.suggested_fix: In the variable declaration: add `validation { condition = length(var.opcert_admin_principal_arns) > 0 && !contains(var.opcert_admin_principal_arns, '*') error_message = 'opcert_admin_principal_arns must be a non-empty list of specific IAM principal ARNs.' }`. In AC1: add a jq sub-claim such as `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type=="aws_iam_role" and .name=="rebar_opcert_admin")] | .[0].values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[] | select(.Principal | (type=="object" and (.AWS=="*" or .Federated=="*")) or .=="*") | error' 2>/dev/null; [ $? -ne 0 ]`

findings.finding: The `rebar-opcert-admin` IAM role's trust policy (restricting which principals may assume the role via `opcert_admin_principal_arns`) is declared as a security property in AC1 but has no structured, self-failing assertion — the only machine-verifiable jq command in AC1 validates the *permission policy* (that exactly one `execute-api:Invoke` grant exists on the named resource), not the trust policy. An IaC defect that set the trust policy to `"*"` or `"arn:aws:iam::*:root"` would pass the AC1 test command while granting any AWS principal the ability to assume the admin role and invoke the opcert endpoint.
findings.location: AC1 — structured jq assertion; What section — 'trust policy limited to the principal ARNs supplied via a new variable opcert_admin_principal_arns'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC1 jq command (or add a second command) such as: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type == "aws_iam_role" and .name == "rebar_opcert_admin")] | length == 1 and (.[0].values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[] | .Principal | to_entries | all(.value != "*"))'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the complete `jq` path and expected value for the `lifecycle.ignore_changes` assertion against `terraform show -json tf.plan`, including the resource address selector and the expected array value (e.g. `jq -e '.configuration.root_module.resources[] | select(.address == "aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_ed25519_key") | .lifecycle.ignore_changes | contains(["value"])'`).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `terraform show -json tf.plan` exposes `lifecycle.ignore_changes` under `.configuration.root_module` and that a `jq -e` query against that path can structurally verify the key parameter's `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` block. However, in Terraform's JSON plan output, `lifecycle` metadata including `ignore_changes` appears under `.configuration.root_module.resources[].lifecycle.ignore_changes` as an array of attribute-name strings — but only when the resource uses a `for_each` iterator does it vary; for a single resource the path is `.configuration.root_module.resources[] | select(.address == "aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_key") | .lifecycle.ignore_changes`. The plan does not specify the exact jq path or the expected array value (e.g. `["value"]`), leaving the assertion underspecified and potentially failing if the path is wrong.
findings.location: AC1 — 'lifecycle.ignore_changes block … `.configuration.root_module` for the key parameter's `lifecycle.ignore_changes` block'

findings.finding: The SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` is explicitly described as the sole copy of the key ('the key exists nowhere else') yet the plan does not declare `prevent_destroy` on it. A `terraform destroy -target` on that parameter — or a state rm + apply — silently and irreversibly destroys the only copy, with no Terraform safeguard.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString; AC1: 'the two SecureString parameters (the key parameter guarded by lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] })'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the new `aws_ssm_parameter` resource for `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, use:
```hcl
lifecycle {
  ignore_changes  = [value]
  prevent_destroy = true
}
```
This matches the existing precedent on `aws_ebs_volume.data` for irreplaceable stateful resources.

findings.finding: The SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` — explicitly described as the only copy of the Ed25519 private key — has `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` to prevent Terraform from overwriting the operator-seeded key, but the plan specifies NO `prevent_destroy` on this parameter. A `terraform destroy`, a state-level `terraform state rm` followed by re-apply, or accidental module deletion would destroy the SSM parameter and permanently lose the key with no recovery path.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`; AC1 — 'the key parameter guarded by lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }'

findings.finding: The private Ed25519 key generation and SSM seeding is an explicitly irreversible operation ("the key exists nowhere else"), but the plan provides no documented recovery procedure for accidental SSM parameter deletion or Terraform state loss — the trust anchor would be permanently broken with no back-out path.
findings.location: ## What — SSM Parameter Store / Deploy + prove section; AC bullet 1 (lifecycle { ignore_changes })
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/runbooks/opcert-key-recovery.md` (or a section in `infra/terraform/README.md`) documenting: (1) detect: service returns signing errors / public key mismatch; (2) recover: `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N "" -f /tmp/opcert-ed25519`; `aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key --value "$(cat /tmp/opcert-ed25519)" --type SecureString --overwrite`; shred local copy; (3) re-pin: update `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` with new public key and new `added_at_log_position`. Reference this runbook from the AC operator-attestation comment template.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the ticket's `file_impact` structured field: replace `infra/opcert/` with `infra/terraform/` and correct the reason to reflect API Gateway HTTP API + SSM Parameter Store (no Fargate, no Secrets Manager).
findings.finding: The ticket's `file_impact` structured metadata lists `infra/opcert/` as the Terraform edit path (with reason 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager'), but this path does not exist in the repo and the plan body explicitly places all new Terraform in the existing `infra/terraform/` directory. The stale reason also contradicts the plan's explicit rejection of Fargate and Secrets Manager.
findings.location: Ticket `file_impact` field / What section (Terraform)
findings.suggested_fix: Change file_impact entry to `{"path": "infra/terraform/", "reason": "Extend with API Gateway HTTP API v2, IAM role rebar-opcert-admin, SSM SecureString parameters, hashicorp/random provider"}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and specify the exact Terraform `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters` key format and value syntax for injecting a static literal header value (`X-Opcert-Guard`), and add a concrete example to the plan or AC1's jq assertion.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the API Gateway HTTP API integration injects a static `X-Opcert-Guard` request header via an `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters` mapping, but never names the Terraform attribute, key format, or value syntax for this mapping. The Terraform AWS provider's `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` uses `request_parameters` with a specific key format (`append:header.<HeaderName>` or `overwrite:header.<HeaderName>`) and value format (`$request.header.<SourceHeader>` or a literal string in a non-obvious form). This is an unverified capability assertion: the plan assumes the feature exists and works as described but provides no attribute-level specification for the executor.
findings.location: What section — Terraform subsection: 'injecting a shared origin-guard header' and 'mapping it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'; AC1 sub-claim 'the `X-Opcert-Guard` static request-parameter mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'The integration uses `request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = "'${random_password.opcert_guard.result}'" }` (or equivalent `append:` form); verify with `terraform plan` that the attribute is accepted before asserting it in AC1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` Terraform attribute name and AWS request-parameter key syntax for static header injection (e.g. `request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.x-opcert-guard" = "..." }`) so the jq AC1 assertion and the implementation agree on the precise field path in the planned JSON.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the origin-guard value can be injected as a static `X-Opcert-Guard` request header via the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource, but neither the exact Terraform attribute name nor the required AWS key-syntax for overwriting a request header are specified. In the `hashicorp/aws` provider, static header injection on an HTTP_PROXY integration uses the `request_parameters` map with keys of the form `"overwrite:header.x-header-name"` (lowercased header, `overwrite:` prefix). A wrong attribute name or key prefix causes silent misconfiguration — the header is never injected and the origin-guard check silently passes through open (or always fails), with no plan-time error.
findings.location: What section — 'the integration URI … injecting a shared origin-guard header'; AC1 — 'the X-Opcert-Guard static request-parameter mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and AC1, name the attribute explicitly: `request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.x-opcert-guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result }` on `aws_apigatewayv2_integration`, and update the AC1 jq path to match `.request_parameters["overwrite:header.x-opcert-guard"]` in `.planned_values`.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource can inject a static `X-Opcert-Guard` request header, but does not cite or verify the exact Terraform attribute name and key format required. The `request_parameters` attribute on `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` uses a non-obvious key syntax (e.g., `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard`) that is distinct from REST API mapping expressions; an incorrect key (e.g., `header.X-Opcert-Guard` or `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard`) is silently accepted by `terraform apply` while never injecting the header — leaving the inner origin-guard perimeter absent with no deploy-time signal. There is zero prior art for `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` anywhere in `infra/terraform/`.
findings.location: What section — 'maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'; AC1 — 'the box-origin integration with the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and AC1, cite the exact HCL: `request_parameters = { "append:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result }` (or the operator-seeded SSM value reference). Add to the AC1 jq command a check that the integration's `request_parameters` map contains the `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` key.

findings.finding: The plan describes injecting the origin-guard as 'a static request-header parameter (X-Opcert-Guard) on the API Gateway integration' but never names the Terraform attribute (`request_parameters`) or the required AWS mapping-key syntax (`"overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result`). The `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource uses a specific colon-delimited mapping syntax for header overrides; an executor who guesses a plausible-sounding attribute name (e.g., `request_headers`) will produce a `terraform validate`-passing file that silently injects nothing.
findings.location: What section — Terraform bullet, origin-guard header provisioning path; AC1 — 'box-origin integration with the static X-Opcert-Guard request-header mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Terraform bullet: 'In the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource, inject the guard header via `request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result }`.' Also add an AC1 test assertion that the plan JSON shows this attribute on the integration resource.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback runbook to the plan (or a referenced doc section) covering: (1) `terraform destroy` order and the nginx template revert needed to remove the include directive before destroy, (2) re-pinning or removing the `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` entry, and (3) the fact that a new Ed25519 key must be generated if the SSM parameter is destroyed.
findings.finding: No rollback procedure is specified: if the deployed API Gateway, nginx config changes, or compose service need to be reverted, the plan provides no explicit steps, leaving the out-of-band-seeded key and the trusted_environments.yaml pinning in an ambiguous state.
findings.location: ## What — Deploy + prove section; Acceptance Criteria AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a '## Rollback' section to the plan: step 1 — revert `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` to remove the `/opcert/` location and include, re-run the nginx install step, and reload HOST nginx (prevents the missing-include failure); step 2 — `terraform destroy -target=aws_apigatewayv2_api.opcert` (or full destroy); step 3 — remove or mark-retired the `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` entry; note that the Ed25519 key is unrecoverable after SSM parameter deletion and a new key+pinning is required for any future re-deploy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `opcert_admin_principal_arns` is supplied at apply time (e.g. a `terraform.tfvars` file tracked in the repo with placeholder ARNs, a `validation` block ensuring the list is non-empty, and a type annotation `type = list(string)`) so the variable is self-documenting and its absence fails loudly.
findings.finding: The `opcert_admin_principal_arns` variable introduced for the `rebar-opcert-admin` role trust policy has no specified default value, type validation, or documentation of how it is supplied at apply time, leaving a critical security variable under-specified.
findings.location: What section — Terraform subsection: 'trust policy limited to the principal ARNs supplied via a new variable `opcert_admin_principal_arns`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the https:// integration URI scheme assertion from AC3 (it is already fully covered by the structured `jq -e` assertion in AC1) and restrict AC3's test scope to compose/nginx/materialize/compose-up wiring only.
findings.finding: AC1 and AC3 both assert that the Terraform integration URI uses the `https://` scheme, via different test mechanisms — AC1 via a `jq -e` query against `terraform show -json tf.plan` (`.planned_values`), and AC3 via a separate unit/integration test. This creates a near-duplicate requirement across two acceptance criteria for the same factual property, which may cause implementers to build redundant test infrastructure or become confused about which AC 'owns' this assertion.
findings.location: AC1 (jq assertions list: '`.planned_values` for resource values: the `https://` integration URI scheme') and AC3 ('a test asserts ... the Terraform integration URI uses the `https://` scheme')
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the clause 'and that the Terraform integration URI uses the `https://` scheme' from AC3. AC1's jq test against `terraform show -json tf.plan` already provides a structured, self-failing assertion for this property.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a version constraint for `hashicorp/random` in `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers` (e.g. `version = "~> 3.0"`) to match the existing `hashicorp/aws ~> 5.0` pinning discipline.
findings.finding: The `hashicorp/random` provider is added to `required_providers` without a version constraint, leaving the resolved version undetermined across environments and future `terraform init` runs.
findings.location: What section — Terraform subsection: 'the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'
findings.suggested_fix:
In `versions.tf` `required_providers`, add:
```hcl
random = {
  source  = "hashicorp/random"
  version = "~> 3.0"
}
```
This follows the existing `~> major.minor` constraint pattern used for `hashicorp/aws`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` runs with `set -euo pipefail`, exits non-zero with a descriptive message on SSM fetch failure, and that `compose-up.sh` treats a non-zero exit as a fatal boot error (halting before `docker compose up`), so the failure is visible rather than silent.
findings.finding: The materialize script's SSM fetch failure mode is not specified: if `aws ssm get-parameter` fails (SSM unavailable, permission error, network timeout), the script's behavior and its effect on `compose-up.sh` are undefined — and no retry or error-surfacing is described.
findings.location: ## What — Compose/nginx section; `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the script spec: `set -euo pipefail`; wrap the `aws ssm get-parameter` call with an explicit error message on failure (`echo 'FATAL: could not fetch opcert-origin-guard from SSM' >&2; exit 1`). In `compose-up.sh`, ensure the materialize call is not backgrounded and its exit code is checked (the existing `set -e` in compose-up.sh covers this if the call is inline).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out section covering at minimum: (a) reverting the nginx template to its pre-story state and re-rendering it, (b) running `terraform destroy` scoped to the new resources (or `terraform state rm` + manual cleanup) if `apply` partially fails, and (c) a safe re-try path if the materialize script fails at boot without taking Gerrit down.
findings.finding: The plan modifies two existing operational files (`infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` and `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh`) on the live production box but provides no explicit rollback or back-out path if the deployment goes wrong — only the boot-ordering hazard is acknowledged, not a recovery procedure.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring; Acceptance Criteria AC3/AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection under Scope or as a separate AC (operator-attested): '(a) nginx: revert `rebar.conf.template` to pre-story state, re-render, `nginx -s reload`; (b) terraform: `terraform destroy -target=aws_apigatewayv2_api.opcert -target=aws_iam_role.rebar_opcert_admin -target=random_password.opcert_guard -target=aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_*`; (c) compose-up.sh: revert the added materialize call and re-deploy.' Also confirm the materialize script is non-fatal on SSM failure (exits 0 with deny-all default intact) so a credentials failure doesn't prevent Gerrit from starting.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC requiring the two-step guard-rotation runbook to be committed as a named file in the repo (e.g., `infra/runbooks/rotate-opcert-guard.md`) and referenced from `materialize-opcert-guard.sh`'s header comment, following the same pattern as the existing materialize scripts.
findings.finding: The two-step guard-rotation runbook ('`terraform apply -replace=…` then re-run `materialize-opcert-guard.sh`') is described only in the plan's prose and has no AC requiring it to land as a durable file in the repo (e.g., `infra/runbooks/rotate-opcert-guard.md`), leaving critical operational knowledge stranded in the story ticket after merge.
findings.location: What section — '(b) ROTATION — the documented runbook is two steps in order…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a key-rotation runbook (analogous to the guard-rotation runbook) documenting: (1) generate new key pair locally, (2) `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` to replace the SecureString, (3) restart/reload the opcert compose service to pick up the new key, (4) update `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` with the new public key + new `added_at_log_position`, (5) revoke/archive the old trusted-environment entry.
findings.finding: The Ed25519 private key SSM parameter carries `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` so Terraform will never update or replace it, yet the plan specifies NO key-rotation runbook. If the private key is compromised or must be rotated on a schedule, the operator has no documented procedure: they must manually overwrite the SSM SecureString, regenerate the key pair, and update `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` with the new public key — but none of these steps are specified, and the guard-rotation runbook (which IS documented) is not analogous because it relies on `terraform apply -replace=` whereas the key parameter explicitly forbids Terraform from touching its value.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store paragraph; `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` on `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Key rotation` subsection under the runbook/deploy section specifying the five steps above. Optionally provide a `rotate-opcert-key.sh` helper script alongside the existing materialize scripts to make the procedure auditable and repeatable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the split-brain recovery procedure for guard rotation: if Step 2 (materialize) fails after Step 1 (terraform apply) succeeds, the operator must re-run `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` using the current SSM value (which already holds the new guard); add a note that re-running Step 2 alone is always safe and idempotent — and optionally make materialize-opcert-guard.sh check the SSM value matches the API Gateway integration parameter as a post-condition sanity check.
findings.finding: The two-step guard-rotation procedure has no documented failure-recovery path for the case where Step 1 succeeds (Terraform updates SSM + API Gateway static header to the new guard value) but Step 2 fails (materialize script errors before writing or reloading nginx). In this split-brain state, API Gateway injects the NEW guard value but nginx still validates against the OLD guard value, causing all `/opcert/` requests to return 403 indefinitely — not just during a brief window but until an operator manually re-runs the materialize script. The plan acknowledges the normal rotation window as "brief and acceptable" but treats it as self-resolving, which is only true when both steps succeed.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring paragraph; rotation runbook: '(b) ROTATION — the documented runbook is two steps in order'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief decommission note (or a `## Teardown` section) specifying: run `terraform destroy` (removes API GW, SSM params, IAM role), remove the `include` and `/opcert/` location blocks from `rebar.conf.template`, delete `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` and reload nginx, and mark the `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` entry as retired/archived.
findings.finding: The plan defines no RETIRE/cleanup path for the host-filesystem artifact `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` or for the entry in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` if the opcert service is ever decommissioned. The Terraform-managed resources (API Gateway, SSM parameters, IAM role) are cleaned up by `terraform destroy`, but the nginx include file and the trusted-environments entry live outside Terraform and have no documented removal procedure.
findings.location: Scope section; What section — Compose/nginx wiring paragraph; AC4 (trusted_environments.yaml)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a deliberate in-scope or out-of-scope statement about `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` BOT_PATHS: either explicitly add `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` to BOT_PATHS as part of this story's compose wiring work, or document in the plan that opcert auto-deploy is deferred and explain the operational consequence.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the autodeploy.sh `BOT_PATHS` variable. Reading `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` line 55 shows `BOT_PATHS='src/rebar/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot pyproject.toml infra/compose/docker-compose.yml'`. The plan adds `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (a sibling story deliverable) and modifies `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`, but the plan is silent on whether `Dockerfile.opcert` needs to be in `BOT_PATHS` for auto-deploy to rebuild the opcert service on source changes. The plan's 'Out of scope' section does not mention autodeploy.sh, so this is an invisible exclusion rather than a deliberate one.
findings.location: Scope section ('Out: … any change to rebar library code'); What section (Compose — extend infra/compose/docker-compose.yml)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` as a required edit target: add `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` to `BOT_PATHS` (line 55) so a Dockerfile.opcert-only change triggers an opcert service rebuild.
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` is an unenumerated consumer. Its `BOT_PATHS` variable (line 55) lists `infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot` as the only Dockerfile redeploy trigger; the plan adds `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (referenced by the new `opcert` service in docker-compose.yml) but does not enumerate `autodeploy.sh` as a file requiring `BOT_PATHS` extension. A `Dockerfile.opcert`-only change will therefore never trigger an autodeploy rebuild of the opcert service.
findings.location: Compose section (What) / `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` line 55
findings.suggested_fix: Change line 55 of autodeploy.sh to: `BOT_PATHS='src/rebar/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert pyproject.toml infra/compose/docker-compose.yml'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct or refine the claim 'an HTTP API has no resource policy': if the choice to omit a resource policy is deliberate (e.g. TF provider limitation or cost of complexity), say so explicitly rather than asserting it is technically impossible.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as a factual technical constraint, using it as the sole justification for why caller restriction is implemented exclusively via identity policy on the IaC-created `rebar-opcert-admin` role. This is an unverified capability assertion: AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs *do* support resource-based policies (set via `PutRestApiPolicy` / `aws apigatewayv2 put-resource-policy`), though the Terraform AWS provider's `aws_apigatewayv2_api` resource does not expose a `policy` argument for HTTP APIs. The claim forecloses a defence-in-depth option without verifying whether it is technically unavailable vs. merely inconvenient.
findings.location: What section — Terraform subsection — API Gateway paragraph: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with the accurate statement: 'the Terraform AWS provider does not support resource-based policies on HTTP API v2 endpoints; identity-policy restriction on the `rebar-opcert-admin` role is the sole access-control mechanism at the API Gateway layer.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the prior-art citation: `materialize-deploy-key.sh` is called from `setup-replication.sh` (not `compose-up.sh`); the actual compose-up.sh precedent is `materialize-g2p-config.sh` alone — update all references to cite only `materialize-g2p-config.sh` as the boot-orchestrator integration precedent.
findings.finding: The plan cites `infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh` as a precedent for wiring the new `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` into `compose-up.sh` ('WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` alongside the existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls'). However, reading `compose-up.sh` confirms that `materialize-deploy-key.sh` is NOT called from `compose-up.sh` at all — it is called from `infra/gerrit/setup-replication.sh` (a separate, operator-run step). The actual precedent for a materialize script called from `compose-up.sh` is `materialize-g2p-config.sh` (line 177). The misidentified prior art may cause the executor to misread the established wiring pattern.
findings.location: What section — Compose subsection: 'WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator `infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` alongside the existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls, running BEFORE `docker compose up` (the precedent ordering)'; AC3: 'per the `infra/gerrit/materialize-*.sh` precedent'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'following the existing precedent (`infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh` / `materialize-g2p-config.sh`)' with 'following the `infra/gerrit/materialize-g2p-config.sh` precedent (the only materialize script currently called from `compose-up.sh`)' throughout the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability for the new opcert infra: at minimum, specify API Gateway HTTP API access logging (CloudWatch Logs destination on the `$default` stage) and a CloudWatch alarm on `5XXError` count for the new API, wired to the existing SNS alerts topic.
findings.finding: No logging, metrics, or alarms are specified for the new API Gateway HTTP API or the `opcert` compose service, leaving the new trust-anchor endpoint unobservable.
findings.location: What section — 'Deploy + prove' and Acceptance Criteria; absence of observability specification for the new API GW / opcert service

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the rationale: HTTP API v2 does support resource-based policies; state the actual design choice ('we choose not to use a resource policy because identity-policy + AWS_IAM SigV4 is sufficient and simpler to manage') rather than the incorrect capability claim.
findings.finding: The plan states 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as the factual basis for the identity-policy-only access-control design (no `aws_apigatewayv2_api` resource policy in IaC). However, AWS did introduce resource-based policies for API Gateway HTTP APIs (as of late 2023). If a resource policy is available but not used, an attacker who can assume any role in the same account (but not `rebar-opcert-admin`) could not invoke the API — but the plan's stated rationale ('an HTTP API has no resource policy') is factually incorrect, potentially causing a future operator to mistakenly add one thinking it contradicts the design or to rely on an incorrect architectural understanding.
findings.location: What section — 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with 'we do not add a resource policy — the `AWS_IAM` route auth with the identity-policy-scoped `rebar-opcert-admin` role provides sufficient restriction without one'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step in the 'Deploy + prove' key-generation procedure to securely delete the local private key file immediately after SSM seeding (e.g., `shred -u` or `rm` on a tmpfs path), or use a pipe/process-substitution approach so the private key is never written to persistent storage.
findings.finding: The Ed25519 private key is generated locally with `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N ""` (which writes a cleartext private-key file to the local filesystem) and then seeded into SSM via the AWS CLI. The plan says 'the key exists nowhere else' as a security property, but provides no instruction to securely delete the local key file after seeding — leaving a cleartext copy of the private key on the operator's workstation, which is a secret-lifecycle gap on the boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: 'Deploy + prove' section: 'generate the key locally (ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N "") and seed the SecureString (the key exists nowhere else)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the two-step generate-then-seed with a pipeline that avoids writing to disk: `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N '' -f /dev/stdin <<< '' 2>/dev/null | aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key --type SecureString --value "$(cat -)"` — or generate to a tmpfs mount and shred immediately: `tmp=$(mktemp -p /dev/shm); ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N '' -f $tmp; aws ssm put-parameter ... --value "$(cat $tmp)"; shred -u $tmp $tmp.pub`.

findings.finding: The live access-control checks in AC5 test only (a) a correctly-signed request from the authorized role and (b) a completely unsigned/unauthenticated request. They do NOT test a SigV4-signed request from a valid-but-wrong AWS identity (i.e., an identity that IS authenticated but is NOT the `rebar-opcert-admin` role). This failure path is the critical one for verifying that the `execute-api:Invoke` grant is correctly scoped to exactly the IaC-created role — a misconfigured trust policy or an overly-broad `execute-api:Invoke` could make the wrong-role request succeed while the plan-level jq check still passes (it checks plan intent, not deployed policy enforcement).
findings.location: AC5 (live checks) and AC1 (Terraform declarations)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC5 with: 'a SigV4-signed request from a valid AWS identity that is NOT the rebar-opcert-admin role is rejected with 403 at API Gateway — evidence: captured response body/status alongside the invoking identity (aws sts get-caller-identity output).' This can be done by signing with the operator's base IAM user/role (before sts:AssumeRole to rebar-opcert-admin), which is already present in the authenticated AWS CLI session.

findings.finding: The boot-safety property — that the nginx config install step ships a DENY-ALL default `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` (so nginx starts fail-closed on `/opcert/` before `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` runs) — is described prominently in What but is not explicitly asserted by any AC. AC #3 asserts that the nginx route requires `X-Opcert-Guard` and that the materialize script renders the guard value, but does not assert the existence or content of the deny-all default file that provides the fail-closed boot guarantee. An implementer could omit this file and satisfy all ACs.
findings.location: AC #3 / What section (boot ordering hazard closure)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC #3, append: 'and that the nginx config installation (not the materialize script) ships a deny-all default `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` containing only `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }` so that `/opcert/` returns 403 if the materialize script has not yet run.'

findings.finding: The fail-closed boot invariant — that a DENY-ALL `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` is installed before nginx starts so a fresh boot serves 403 on /opcert/ rather than nginx failing on a missing include — is explicitly specified in the What section as a safety property, but no Acceptance Criterion asserts that this default file is part of the nginx-config installation artifacts or that the template install step ships it.
findings.location: AC3 (compose/nginx test assertions); What section — 'Two ordering hazards are closed explicitly: (a) BOOT'
findings.suggested_fix: In the compose/nginx test (AC3), add two assertions: (1) parse `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` and assert it contains `include /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf;` and a `location /opcert/` block with the guard check; (2) assert that the nginx-config install script (or a tracked file at `infra/nginx/opcert-guard.conf.default` or similar) ships the deny-all map content `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }` — confirming the fail-closed default is part of the artifact set, not just documented intent.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `materialize-deploy-key.sh` is called from `compose-up.sh` as a precedent for wiring `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` into the boot orchestrator ('WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator infra/scripts/compose-up.sh alongside the existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls, running BEFORE docker compose up' — citing 'infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh'). Reading compose-up.sh confirms only `materialize-g2p-config.sh` is called there (line 177); `materialize-deploy-key.sh` is invoked from `infra/gerrit/setup-replication.sh`, not compose-up.sh. The cited two-script precedent is half-wrong, which means the plan's claim that the new script follows an established wiring pattern in compose-up.sh overstates the precedent.
findings.location: What section — Compose subsection: 'It is WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator infra/scripts/compose-up.sh alongside the existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls, running BEFORE docker compose up (the precedent ordering)'; also AC3: 'The test also asserts infra/scripts/compose-up.sh calls the materialize script before docker compose up'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the precedent ordering' citation from 'infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh' to 'infra/gerrit/materialize-g2p-config.sh' (the actual compose-up.sh precedent); note that materialize-deploy-key.sh follows a different invocation path (setup-replication.sh).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a new ADR (e.g. `docs/adr/0048-opcert-apigw-host-nginx-origin-guard.md`) capturing the SigV4 + HOST-nginx origin-guard two-layer auth pattern, the rejected alternatives (VPC link, Fargate, NLB), and the cost/isolation rationale, and reference it from the Terraform source.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel two-layer authentication architecture (API Gateway HTTP API SigV4 + HOST-nginx origin-guard header) that was explicitly chosen over alternatives (VPC link, Fargate, NLB) but no new ADR is declared to capture this decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: ## Why / ## What (Terraform section) / Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0048-opcert-apigw-host-nginx-origin-guard.md` with status Accepted, context (zero fixed-cost constraint, ADR 0046 L4 isolation requirement), decision (HTTP API SigV4 + HOST-nginx guard header), and consequences (rotation runbook, no VPC link needed). Reference it in a comment at the top of the new API Gateway Terraform file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the compose service spec: a `restart: unless-stopped` (or `always`) policy, a `healthcheck` (e.g. HTTP GET /health or TCP probe), and specify the log driver (e.g. `awslogs` to an existing CloudWatch log group, or `json-file` with log rotation); and add at minimum a CloudWatch alarm (or a note that existing host-level monitoring covers it) for sustained 5xx from the API Gateway integration.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for the opcert compose service: no health-check directive, no restart policy, no CloudWatch log group or container log driver, and no alert for the service being down — leaving operators blind to failures of this trust-anchor service.
findings.location: ## What — Compose section; `infra/compose/docker-compose.yml`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the origin-guard value must be Terraform-managed via `hashicorp/random` (if API Gateway's `request_parameters` cannot reference an SSM-sourced `aws_ssm_parameter` data source or a static local — the two aws-provider-only alternatives), or document why the existing CHANGEME + operator-seed pattern is insufficient, to justify adding a new provider at 0 existing call-sites.
findings.finding: `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) is introduced as a new Terraform provider dependency with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three: 0/3), while the codebase's established pattern for all 12 existing secrets is CHANGEME placeholder + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` + operator-seeded via `aws ssm put-parameter` — an approach that already exists in the AWS provider with no new dependency. The functional distinction claimed (Terraform must own and inject the value into the API Gateway static-header parameter) is real, but the same outcome is achievable within the existing `hashicorp/aws` provider toolchain.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password`… the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'
findings.suggested_fix: If the guard value only needs to be consistent between the API Gateway integration and the SSM parameter, consider using `aws_ssm_parameter` (data source read-back) or a `random_id` resource (already within `hashicorp/random` — same trade-off) vs. seeding a locally-generated value via a `null_resource` local-exec `openssl rand -hex 32 | aws ssm put-parameter …` in the same apply, matching the established operator-seed pattern. If `random_password` is genuinely the cleanest approach, add a one-sentence justification in the plan for why the existing aws-provider-only pattern cannot close the loop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the leaf's `file_impact` field to remove the stale `infra/opcert/` / 'Fargate + Secrets Manager' entry and replace it with the correct paths (`infra/terraform/`, `infra/compose/`, `infra/scripts/`, `infra/nginx/`, `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`) consistent with the parent's settled runtime decision and the leaf's own plan body.
findings.finding: The leaf ticket's `file_impact` structured field lists `infra/opcert/` with reason 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager', which contradicts both the parent epic's explicit rejection of Fargate/Secrets Manager AND the leaf's own plan body. The parent's cost-verified runtime decision (recorded in its comments and body) explicitly rejects Fargate, Secrets Manager, and KMS CMK; the parent's revised decomposition item 3 targets `infra/terraform/` and `infra/compose/`, not `infra/opcert/`. This stale metadata names out-of-scope resources (Fargate, Secrets Manager) that the parent declared rejected, and an incorrect path (`infra/opcert/` vs `infra/terraform/`).
findings.location: ticket 76d2-00c3-88e8-4654, `file_impact` field — entry `{path: 'infra/opcert/', reason: 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager'}`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the file_impact entry `{path: 'infra/opcert/', reason: 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager'}` with: `{path: 'infra/terraform/', reason: 'API Gateway HTTP API + rebar-opcert-admin IAM role + SSM SecureString parameters + hashicorp/random provider'}, {path: 'infra/compose/', reason: 'opcert compose service + Dockerfile.opcert (sibling story)'}, {path: 'infra/scripts/', reason: 'materialize-opcert-guard.sh + compose-up.sh extension'}, {path: 'infra/nginx/', reason: 'rebar.conf.template /opcert/ location + opcert-guard.conf include'}, {path: '.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml', reason: 'pin deployed env public key + env_id with added_at_log_position'}`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `hashicorp/random` + Terraform-state storage of the origin-guard value is preferred over operator-seeding it (consistent with all 12 existing SSM SecureStrings), or switch to the established `value = "CHANGEME" + lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` pattern and document the guard-seed procedure in the deploy steps.
findings.finding: `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) is introduced as a new Terraform provider with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three: 0/3) to generate the origin-guard shared secret, while the codebase's established prior art for all 12 SSM SecureString values is operator-seeded out-of-band. The plan does not justify why `random_password` (which stores the secret cleartext in Terraform state JSON) is preferred over operator-seeding the guard value the same way as every other secret — or over a shell-based `openssl rand -hex 32` seeded once (matching the existing pattern). The only justification given is that the guard value 'IS `random_password.result`' (Terraform-managed), not that this is better than the alternative.
findings.location: What section — Terraform subsection — origin-guard provisioning path; `infra/terraform/versions.tf` (no `hashicorp/random` present); `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` (all 12 secrets are operator-seeded)
findings.suggested_fix: Use `aws ssm put-parameter` to operator-seed the guard value (consistent with all other secrets), declare the SSM parameter with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`, and reference the SSM value directly in the API Gateway integration parameter (via a `data.aws_ssm_parameter` lookup or Terraform parameter substitution). This eliminates the `hashicorp/random` provider dependency and keeps all secrets out of Terraform state.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
